Q1: I tried to keep a pretty structured routine, honestly. I'd block out around 30 hours a week for studying, usually spreading it across mornings and late afternoons. I'd read through materials, take notes by hand, and then type them up later. The problem was that even with all that time invested, I struggled to translate my understanding into exam performance. I kept telling myself the routine would pay off eventually.
Q2: I tried to maintain regular times, but deadlines definitely threw things off. When assignments were due, I'd shift everything around and sometimes pull longer sessions. My anxiety would spike before exams, so I'd add extra study blocks then too. But generally, I preferred consistency because it helped me feel more in control of my situation.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check for new announcements or to see if there were updated materials for the week. Sometimes it was just to watch the lecture videos or participate in discussions. When I was feeling particularly anxious about falling behind, I'd log in just to reassure myself that I hadn't missed anything important. The notifications on my phone also prompted me quite a bit.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, logging in became almost automatic. I'd do it first thing in the morning with my coffee, just scrolling through to see what was new. It became part of how I started my day. I think the discussion posts helped with that because I wanted to stay involved with my peers and not feel isolated in my studying.
Q5: The video lectures were really valuable because I could pause and rewatch sections I didn't understand. The discussion forums were also helpful because seeing how other students interpreted the material gave me different perspectives. I'm a read-write learner, so any downloadable PDFs or lecture notes were gold for me. I'd print them out and annotate them heavily.
Q6: Some of the optional quizzes I skipped because they felt like extra stress without contributing to my grade. There were also some supplementary readings that I kept meaning to get to but never did. I think I avoided them because I was already overwhelmed trying to master the core content. When you're anxious, adding more feels impossible even when it might help.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to what would be assessed or when it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. If I finished something and felt like I learned or could apply it, that was meaningful. But if it felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my goals, my motivation dropped quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, honestly. Required ones had this weight to them that made me prioritize them even when I was exhausted. Optional stuff felt like it was for students who had everything else figured out already. I always told myself I'd circle back to them, but with my anxiety, the required work consumed all my mental energy.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything and studied from printed notes and my own handwritten summaries constantly. I also used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts that weren't clicking. I had a few textbooks from the library that I referenced regularly. A lot of my deepest learning happened away from the screen, just me with my notes and highlighters.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours going through my printed materials, making flashcards, or rewriting concepts in my own words. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I also did study sessions with a couple of classmates in person where we'd quiz each other. That peer interaction was valuable but completely invisible to the LMS.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or watch a video once, then work from the downloaded version for days or even weeks. It was more convenient and didn't require internet. I could study on the train or in the library without worrying about connectivity. So my one login might represent hours of subsequent offline engagement.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. My anxiety was through the roof, and I started feeling sad about my performance despite all my effort. The CBT sessions helped me process some of that, but there were days when I just couldn't bring myself to engage properly. Family stuff was also demanding attention, which split my focus badly.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars and part-time commitments meant some weeks I was running on empty. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those modules temporarily because facing them felt overwhelming.
Q14: Yes, more than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about exams or other life stuff. I'd scroll through readings without really processing the words. It looked like engagement on paper, but internally I was just going through the motions to feel like I was doing something.
Q15: I postponed the bigger assignments even though I knew they mattered most. The anxiety around them was paralyzing. I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better first, but really I was avoiding the fear of failing. It's frustrating because I knew the pattern was harmful, but breaking it felt impossible in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines created these intense bursts of activity for me. I'd be relatively steady, then spike dramatically as due dates approached. The pressure helped me focus but also increased my anxiety significantly. I wish I could have spread the work more evenly, but my brain seemed to need that urgency to overcome the avoidance.
Q17: Feedback was huge for me. Positive feedback gave me hope and motivated me to keep pushing. Critical feedback was harder to take but ultimately useful. The problem was that feedback sometimes came late, so I'd already moved on or made the same mistakes elsewhere. Timely feedback would have helped me adjust faster.
Q18: The weekly structure helped me plan, but the pacing sometimes felt relentless. New content kept coming whether I'd mastered the previous material or not. I appreciated having clear modules to follow, but I often felt like I was chasing the course rather than learning alongside it. More flexibility would have helped me consolidate better.
Q19: The clear organization of materials made engagement easier because I always knew where to find things. However, the sheer volume of content made it harder to prioritize. Also, some assessment criteria were unclear, which increased my anxiety about whether I was focusing on the right things. Clearer guidance on what mattered most would have helped.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying hard but struggling to produce quality work. They're engaging with materials, watching videos, reading everything, but the actual assignments feel too difficult or unclear. It could also be perfectionism or anxiety making them hesitate to submit anything less than perfect. I relate to that pattern somewhat.
Q21: That makes sense to me because some students learn efficiently offline or have strong prior knowledge. They might only need brief LMS contact to get what they need. I tried to be efficient too, but my exam scores didn't reflect my effort. So I guess it depends on how well someone can translate their learning into assessed performance.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like login frequency and time spent, which indicate presence. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone is deeply focused or mindlessly scrolling. They also miss all offline studying, emotional state, and whether the student actually understood anything. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and actively working toward that goal. It's not just showing up but being mentally present and curious. For me, it also means persisting even when things get hard, which I tried to do despite my struggles. True engagement is internal and can't always be measured externally.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I genuinely cared about this course or how hard I fought against my anxiety to keep going. It wouldn't capture the hours of offline study, the peer discussions, or the emotional toll of failing despite real effort. My resilience kept me coming back, but the data just shows incomplete tasks and a failing grade. That's not the whole story.
